title: Square Roots and the Number Line

data.text: Square Roots and the Number Line

Some square roots, such as \(\sqrt{4}\) or \(\sqrt{9}\), simplify to integers. Others, such as \(\sqrt{2}\) and \(\sqrt{5}\), are irrational. Even when the decimal never ends, these values can still be placed exactly on the number line.

data.title: Worked example
data.solution: \(\sqrt{25}=5\), so it is rational. \(\sqrt{5}\) does not simplify to an integer and is irrational.

data.title: Extension note
data.body: This is a useful bridge to later lessons on square-root representation and square-root spirals.

clarifiers.title: Quick rule
clarifiers.body: Perfect square under the root → rational result. Non-perfect square under the root → usually irrational in this chapter.

questions.prompt: Why is the square root of 25 rational, but the square root of 26 irrational?
questions.answer: 25 is a perfect square (5x5), yielding an integer. 26 is not, yielding an infinite non-repeating decimal.
questions.explanation: Perfect squares have integer roots. Other numbers do not.
